source.title: Sensitivity to social contingencies between 1 and 3 months of age

confidence_rationale: This study provides a developmental onset boundary for continuous social contingency monitoring: 1-month-olds do not discriminate Normal, Non-Contingent, and Imitation interaction conditions; 3-month-olds do (gazing longer in imitation, smiling more in normal vs. non-contingent). The developmental transition between 1 and 3 months pins the emergence of the monitoring architecture to a narrow window, before pretend play, theory-of-mind, or language. High quality journal, within-study comparison design, clean manipulation. Directly addresses when the monitoring capacity comes online.
failure_signatures: Single lab, relatively small N per age group.

The three conditions (Normal, Non-Contingent, Imitation) conflate contingency and timing -- Imitation is contingent but time-delayed, making it hard to isolate what dimension 3-month-olds are sensitive to.
Does not track within-episode dynamics -- cannot determine whether monitoring is continuous or periodic sampling.

mapping.source_claim_statement: One-month-old infants do not discriminate contingent from non-contingent or imitation interaction conditions. Three-month-olds discriminate all three: they smile more in normal contingent interaction and gaze longer in the imitation condition. This marks a developmental transition in social contingency sensitivity between 1 and 3 months.
mapping.ree_translation: Pins the emergence of continuous frame monitoring to a narrow developmental window (1-3 months) -- before any sophisticated cognitive capacity. For MECH-197 (play type progression): if the monitoring architecture is already in place by 3 months, then the progression from sensorimotor to functional to constructive to pretend to games-with-rules play does NOT switch on monitoring -- it escalates what monitoring tracks (from contingency-of-response to content-of-game to rules-of-game). The architecture is constant; the register content grows. For Q-035: the heartbeat signal is present in the simplest play; only the frequency and semantic complexity of what it checks changes across development.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The imitation condition introduces its own complexity (social mirroring versus contingent response). The study operationalises contingency as conditional probability of maternal response, not as a formal frame-monitoring task. Extrapolation to REE's play frame concept requires theoretical bridge work.
mapping.source_context: Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology, Leipzig. N=48 across two studies (1-month, 3-month). Face-to-face interaction in three conditions.

confidence_components.notes: Developmental Science is high-quality. Study design is clean with within-study comparison across ages. The developmental boundary (onset between 1-3 months) is the most precise published estimate of when the monitoring capacity emerges.
